title: Postpartum Black Vaginal Discharge in Cats: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Understand why your cat has black discharge after giving birth, when it is an emergency, and how photos help your vet provide the right care.
content_html:
Understanding Postpartum Discharge in Cats

After a cat gives birth, it is normal for her to experience some vaginal discharge, known as lochia. This substance consists of remaining placental tissue and blood. While lochia is typically dark green or reddish-black during the first 24 to 48 hours, it should gradually decrease in volume and change in color. If the discharge is thick, persistent, or has a strong, unpleasant odor, it may indicate an underlying health issue.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. While dark discharge can be part of the natural healing process immediately after labor, it becomes an emergency if it is foul-smelling or if your cat shows signs of illness. If the mother cat is lethargic, refuses to eat, develops a fever, or stops caring for her kittens, she may have metritis (a severe uterine infection) or a retained placenta. These conditions are life-threatening and require immediate veterinary intervention.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photograph of the discharge can be a vital tool for your veterinary triage team. Visual evidence allows a professional to assess the color, opacity, and consistency of the fluid, which are key indicators of whether the discharge is physiological (normal) or pathological (infected). Providing a photo helps the vet determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and prepares the clinic for her arrival.

faqs.question: How long should discharge last after my cat gives birth?
faqs.answer: Normal lochia can persist for up to three weeks, but it should significantly taper off after the first few days and should never have a foul smell.

faqs.question: What does an infection look like postpartum?
faqs.answer: Infections often produce discharge that is thick, pus-like, or has a very strong, offensive odor, often accompanied by a mother cat who appears weak or feverish.

faqs.question: Can a retained placenta cause black discharge?
faqs.answer: Yes, a retained placenta can lead to serious infection and decomposition within the uterus, resulting in dark, abnormal discharge and systemic illness.
